sections.title: What are auxiliary verbs?
sections.content: **Auxiliary verbs** (helping verbs) are used with a main verb to form questions, negatives, emphasis, and compound tenses. **Primary auxiliaries** in English are **be** (*am, is, are, was, were, been*), **have** (*have, has, had*), and **do** (*do, does, did*). In English we **cannot** say *I not know* or *She likes not* — we need an auxiliary: *I do not know. She does not like it.*

sections.title: Do, does, did
sections.content:
**Do / does / did** form questions and negatives in the **present simple** and **past simple**:
Present: *Do you know? She doesn't like it.* (Third person: **does / doesn't**.)
Past: *Did they come? I didn't see him.*
After **do**, **does**, or **did**, always use the **base form** of the main verb (*go, know, like*), not the past or -s form.

sections.title: Be and have
sections.content:
**Be** is used for continuous tenses (*be + -ing*): *I am working. She was leaving.* Also for passives (*be + past participle*): *The letter was sent.*
**Have** is used for perfect tenses (*have + past participle*): *I have finished. She had left.*
Modals (*will, can, must*, etc.) are a separate group — see the Modal Verbs topic.

sections.title: Common mistakes
sections.content:
**Frequent errors with do/does/did** (consolidated):

- Omitting the auxiliary: Wrong *I not understand* → Right *I do not understand* / *I don't understand*
- Third-person form: Wrong *He don't know* → Right *He doesn't know*
- Base form after did/does: Wrong *Did he went?* / *She doesn't knows* → Right *Did he go?* / *She doesn't know*
- Have = possess in questions: Wrong *Have you a car?* → Right *Do you have a car?* (or *Have you got...?* in BrE)
- Word order: Wrong *Why you did go?* → Right *Why did you go?*

**Why it matters**: These are core A1–A2 verb phrase errors. Fixing them improves questions, negatives, and all simple tenses immediately.

sections.title: Reference & next steps
sections.content:
Modals are a separate group (see Modal Verbs topic).

For more on do/does/did in questions/negatives and agreement, see the Verb–Subject Agreement topic.

questions.question: Which auxiliary do we use for questions and negatives in the **present simple**?
questions.explanation: **Do** (I/you/we/they) and **does** (he/she/it) are used for questions and negatives in the present simple.

questions.question: Why is *She don't like it* wrong?

options.a: We must use 'doesn't' with he/she/it in present simple

questions.explanation: Third person singular (he/she/it) in present simple uses **does** / **doesn't**, not *do* / *don't*.

questions.question: After *did* in a question or negative, which form of the main verb do we use?
questions.explanation: After **did** we always use the **base form** of the verb: *Did he go?* not *Did he went?*

questions.question: Which is correct for *Have you a car?* (when 'have' means possess)?
questions.explanation: When **have** is a main verb (possess), we usually form questions with **Do you have...?** In British English *Have you got...?* is also common.

questions.question: Why is *I not know* wrong in English?

options.d: In present simple negative we need the auxiliary **do** + not

questions.explanation: English requires the auxiliary **do** in present simple negatives: *I **do** not know* / *I don't know*.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: After **did** we use the **base form**: go.

questions.question: Which is correct? (have = possess)
questions.explanation: For *have* as main verb (possess), we use **Do you have...?**

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Plural **they** → **don't**; after auxiliary, main verb is base form **have**.
